body_txt: Sometimes when the heroes defeat the Big Bad , they go down gracefully . Sometimes they tell the hero to Get It Over With . Sometimes they try to take the heroes down with them . Sometimes the villain is so badass they defy the hero to the last breath rather than surrender or accept mercy. Some are twisted enough to actively demand that the hero slay them . This trope is not about those villains . Smug Snakes , Dirty Cowards , and bad guys who suffer from Villain Decay rarely go down with their heads held high. For all the bravado and confidence they usually project, they are much more likely to get on their hands and knees and beg for mercy. This also serves to contrast them with the hero, as heroes usually don't beg for mercy from the villain in the opposite situation, at least not for themselves. Very rarely, it's the act of a Noble Demon who is consistent about offering mercy to enemies and expects the favour to be returned. Depending on where the hero is on the Sliding Scale of Idealism Versus Cynicism , they might call the villain out on how many people they've denied mercy before sparing them anyway . Others may declare that the villain is Not Worth Killing or leave them alive as a Cruel Mercy . Or they might just scoff and put the villain down for good. If the hero does grant them mercy, they may perform a Heel–Face Turn out of gratitude. However, it's very common for the villain to attempt to attack the hero one last time , usually while their back is turned . This will rarely work . If the villain doesn't backstab, I Owe You My Life may follow (and because Redemption Equals Death , there's a high chance they'll end up actually giving their life for the hero at some point). It doesn't always lead up to something, though; the villain may just realize they've lost and leave before the hero changes his or her mind . In popular works, such as James Bond and such, where killing the villain is considered standard operating policy, you will rarely, if ever, actually see someone beg for mercy and then be killed by the hero as the resulting Mood Whiplash would be too great and might result in the audience turning against the hero. Of course, there are always Darker and Edgier exceptions, such as with 24 and its licenced-to-kill lead character Jack Bauer. If the villain has been particularly merciless, then the refusal to give the requested mercy can be played up as a heroic moment, as the villain has just proven themselves to be a hypocritical coward who deserves whatever punishment the hero dishes out to them. When the hero does this, it is Ain't Too Proud to Beg . Also see Please, I Will Do Anything! . Compare I Surrender, Suckers! if the villain uses the surrender to trick the hero. Contrast a version of Embarrassing Rescue where the hero tries to Save the Villain but the villain refuses, preferring to die rather than accept help from their enemy. If the villain begs to be incarcerated to escape someone rather than begging for their life, it falls under Prefer Jail to the Protagonist . Compare Entitled Bastard , where a less-malevolent but still repugnant person expects the people they've mistreated or wronged to help them.
